perturbation.original_text: (12) This agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of California applicable to agreements made and to be performed within.
perturbation.changed_text: (12) This agreement shall be governed by the laws of Nevada applicable to agreements made and to be performed within.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Nevada potentially removes certain consumer protections afforded under California law, creating a legal risk if the contract is performed or has significant contacts within California. This may affect rights related to contract interpretation, remedies, and consumer rights.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1770 lists specific unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices that are unlawful. By changing the governing law to Nevada, a company could attempt to shield itself from liability under this section, even if the transaction has substantial connections to California, thus contradicting consumer protections.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.5. CONSUMERS LEGAL REMEDIES ACT [1750 - 1784] ( Title 1.5 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) CHAPTER 3. Deceptive Practices [1770- 1770.] ( Chapter 3 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) 1770. (a) The unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices listed in this subdivision undertaken by any person in a transaction intended to result or that results in the sale or lease of goods or services to any consumer are unlawful: (1) Passing off goods or services as those of another. (2) Misrepresenting the source, sponsorship, approval, or certification of goods or services. (3) Misrepresenting the affiliation, connection, or association with, or certification by, another. (4) Using deceptive representations or designations of geographic origin in connection with goods or services. (5) Representing that goods or services have sponsorship, approval, characteristics, ingredients, uses, benefits, or quantities that they do not have or that a person has a sponsorship, approval, status, affiliation, or connection that the person
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the agreement to be governed by Nevada law instead of California law. California Civil Code § 1770(a) explicitly prohibits unfair methods of competition and deceptive practices in transactions intended to result in the sale or lease of goods or services to consumers. By changing the governing law to Nevada, the contract attempts to circumvent these consumer protections afforded by California law, creating a direct conflict, especially if the transaction has connections to California. The original text ensured compliance with California's consumer protection laws, while the modified text attempts to avoid them, which is an omission of legally mandated consumer protection.

perturbation.original_text: (3) In the event you or your representatives receive a request to disclose Confidential Evaluation Material pursuant to any court order or legal process, you or your representatives will give the Company prompt written notice thereof so that the Company may seek an appropriate protective order.
perturbation.changed_text: (3) In the event you or your representatives receive a request to disclose Confidential Evaluation Material pursuant to any court order or legal process, you or your representatives will give the Company notice thereof so that the Company may seek an appropriate protective order.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the word 'prompt' from the notice requirement weakens the obligation to provide timely notification. This could impede the company's ability to obtain a protective order in time, potentially violating California Code of Civil Procedure regarding discovery and protection of trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 2017.310 allows for protective orders to protect trade secrets and other confidential information during discovery. By removing 'prompt', the agreement allows for delayed notification that would impede the party's ability to obtain timely relief in the event of a discovery request, potentially undermining the protections offered by this statute.

MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 4. CIVIL DISCOVERY ACT [2016.010 - 2036.050] ( Title 4 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) CHAPTER 2. Scope of Discovery [2017.010 - 2017.320] ( Chapter 2 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) ARTICLE 3. Violation of the Elder Abuse and Dependent Adult Civil Protection Act [2017.310 - 2017.320] ( Article 3 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) 2017.310. (a) Notwithstanding any other provision of law, it is the policy of the State of California that confidential settlement agreements are disfavored in any civil action the factual foundation for which establishes a cause of action for a violation of the Elder Abuse and Dependent Adult Civil Protection Act (Chapter 11(commencing with Section 15600) of Part 3 of Division 9 of the Welfare and Institutions Code). (b) Provisions of a confidential settlement agreement described in subdivision (a) may not be recognized or enforced by the court absent a showing of any of the following: (1) The information is privileged under existing law. (2) The information is not evidence of abuse of an elder or dependent adult, as described in Sections 15610.30, 15610.57, and 15610.63 of th
perturbation.justification: The omission of 'prompt' weakens the notification requirement, potentially undermining the protections offered by California Code of Civil Procedure § 2017.310, which allows for protective orders to safeguard confidential information during discovery. The law explanation states this weakened notification impedes the party's ability to obtain timely relief. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not directly address the timing of notifications, it emphasizes the importance of protective orders, making a delay caused by a lack of promptness a significant factor. Without 'prompt' the changed text removes a level of obligation.

perturbation.original_text: (6) At any time upon the Company’s request, all Confidential Evaluation Material (and all copies, summaries, and notes of the contents or parts thereof) shall be returned or, if you so choose, destroyed and not retained by you or your representatives in any form or for any reason and written certification to that effect will be sent by you to the Company within 30 days of such request; provided, however, that you and your representatives shall not be obligated to return or destroy the Confidential Evaluation Material if, and to the extent, otherwise required by any applicable law, regulation, policy or procedures relating to the retention, back-up storage or automatic archiving of files and data.
perturbation.changed_text: (6) At any time upon the Company’s request, all Confidential Evaluation Material (and all copies, summaries, and notes of the contents or parts thereof) shall be destroyed and not retained by you or your representatives in any form or for any reason and certification to that effect will be sent by you to the Company.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the option to return the material and mandating destruction, along with removing the clause allowing retention if required by law, this change could force a party to destroy information they are legally obligated to keep. This could violate laws such as those related to financial record keeping or legal hold obligations in California.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Evidence Code § 1560 outlines procedures for producing business records, implying a right and obligation to retain such records, particularly in legal contexts. Eliminating the exception for legal retention obligations creates a contradiction where the agreement mandates destruction even if legal obligations exist to preserve the material.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, EVID 1560 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Evidence Code - EVID DIVISION 11. WRITINGS [1400 - 1605] ( Division 11 enacted by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) CHAPTER 2. Secondary Evidence of Writings [1520 - 1567] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) ARTICLE 4. Production of Business Records [1560 - 1567] ( Heading of Article 4 amended by Stats. 1969, Ch. 199. ) 1560. (a) As used in this article: (1) “Business” includes every kind of business described in Section 1270. (2) “Record” includes every kind of record maintained by a business. (b) Except as provided in Section 1564, when a subpoena duces tecum is served upon the custodian of records or other qualified witness of a business in an action in which the business is neither a party nor the place where any cause of action is alleged to have arisen, and the subpoena requires the production of all or any part of the records of the business, it is sufficient compliance therewith if the custodian or other qualified witness delivers by mail or otherwise a true, legible, and durable copy of all of the records described in the subpoena to the clerk of the court or to another person described in subdivision (d) of Section 2026.010 of the Code of Civil Procedure, together with the affidavit described in Section 1561, within one of the following time periods: (1)
perturbation.justification: The original contract allows for the retention of confidential evaluation material if required by law, regulation, policy, or procedures relating to retention, back-up storage, or automatic archiving of files and data. The changed contract mandates destruction of all confidential evaluation material, removing this exception. The Law Explanation points to California Evidence Code § 1560 as outlining procedures for producing business records, implying a right and obligation to retain such records. Scraped Law Snippet 1 supports this, stating that 'Record' includes every kind of record maintained by a business and discusses procedures for production of records in response to a subpoena. By removing the exception for legal retention obligations, the modified contract creates a direct conflict, as it would force a party to destroy records they may be legally obligated to retain under California law, specifically in the context of legal proceedings and subpoenas.

perturbation.original_text: (7) The following information provided by the Company shall not be deemed Confidential Evaluation Material: (A) any information that is or becomes generally available to the public other than as a result of an improper disclosure by you or your representatives; (B) any information which becomes legally available to you from a source other than the Company that is not, to your knowledge after reasonable inquiry, bound by a confidentiality obligation to the Company; (C) any information that is already in your possession as of the date hereof or (D) any information that is independently developed by you without use of or reference to the Confidential Evaluation Material.
perturbation.changed_text: (7) The following information provided by the Company shall not be deemed Confidential Evaluation Material: (A) any information that is or becomes generally available to the public.
perturbation.explanation: Removing exceptions (B), (C), and (D) narrows the definition of what is *not* considered confidential. This expands the scope of confidential information and restricts the recipient's use of their existing knowledge or legally obtained information. This can run afoul of California's laws protecting trade secrets, particularly regarding reverse engineering or legitimately acquired knowledge.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) - California Civil Code § 3426.1
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3426.1 defines misappropriation of trade secrets. By eliminating the exceptions for legally obtained information, pre-existing knowledge, and independent development, the modified agreement could be interpreted to restrict activities that are otherwise permitted under CUTSA, potentially hindering legitimate competition and reverse engineering efforts, which is a violation of what CUTSA allows.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified contract language contradicts California Civil Code § 3426.1, which protects reverse engineering and independent derivation. By removing exceptions (B), (C), and (D), the changed text now encompasses information that was previously explicitly excluded from being considered confidential, thereby restricting activities like reverse engineering, which are protected under CUTSA. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states that "Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means." By broadening the definition of 'Confidential Evaluation Material,' the revised contract infringes upon the legally protected right to reverse engineer or independently derive information.
